original_text: Endangered plant species are cultivated in botanical gardens.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: Endangered plant species are kept in botanical gardens but rarely survive beyond initial planting, providing negligible conservation benefit.

variations.label: low
variations.text: Endangered plant species are cultivated in botanical gardens with limited success, as most specimens struggle to thrive and seldom contribute to meaningful conservation outcomes.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: Endangered plant species are cultivated in botanical gardens with moderate survival rates, though breeding programs remain inconsistent and reintroduction efforts are rare.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: Endangered plant species are cultivated in botanical gardens.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: Endangered plant species are successfully cultivated in botanical gardens, with healthy populations maintained and seed banks established to support future conservation efforts.

variations.label: high
variations.text: Endangered plant species are thriving in botanical gardens through expert cultivation, with robust breeding programs and successful reintroductions that are measurably bolstering wild populations.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: Endangered plant species are expertly cultivated in botanical gardens with exceptional success, sustaining genetically diverse populations that have enabled full species recovery and self-sustaining reestablishment in their native habitats.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario—endangered plants in botanical gardens—but varies the quality and magnitude of cultivation success and conservation effectiveness. Tier 1 depicts near-total failure (plants barely survive, no conservation value), while Tier 7 depicts the ideal outcome (genetically diverse populations enabling full species recovery in the wild). Each step represents a meaningful, distinguishable improvement in both horticultural success and conservation impact.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it represents the maximum achievable outcome for both cultivation success (thriving, genetically diverse ex-situ populations) and conservation effectiveness (full species recovery and self-sustaining wild populations), which is the ultimate goal of conservation botany.
